title: Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Net](#dod:net-oda) [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita

Net official development assistance (ODA), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

This scatter plot maps countries based on their GDP per capita on the horizontal axis and net official development assistance (ODA) given per capita on the vertical axis. The horizontal axis ranges from $0 to $120,000 in 2021 international dollars, while the vertical axis spans from $0 to over $800 in constant 2023 US dollars. Data points are color-coded by region: North America (orange), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue), with circle sizes representing population. A general positive correlation is visible, where countries with higher GDP per capita tend to provide more aid per person. Norway sits at the highest point on the vertical axis, exceeding $800 in aid per capita with a GDP per capita near $90,000. Luxembourg and Ireland are positioned at the far right, with GDP per capita figures approaching or exceeding $110,000 and aid levels between $400 and $500. Switzerland and Sweden cluster in the upper-middle section, showing aid levels between $400 and $500 against GDP figures around $70,000 to $80,000. The United States is represented by a large orange circle, indicating a high population, positioned near $80,000 GDP per capita and roughly $200 in aid. Asian nations like Thailand, Turkey, and Kazakhstan occupy the lower-left quadrant, with GDP per capita below $40,000 and aid contributions under $100. Japan and South Korea appear in the middle range, with GDP figures between $40,000 and $60,000 and aid levels generally below $200. Qatar is an outlier in the high-income, moderate-aid range, located past $100,000 GDP with aid near $250.
